messages.content: please help me to polish the following paragraph. requirements: 1. try to make the logic and language to make them strict and scientific 2. when polishing, try only to change the sentences’s order or replace phrases but do not delete any actual content. 3. note, it is very important not to delete any actual content when polishing. the paragraph to be polished is: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, particularly ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations utilizing quantum mechanical force fields, offer a promising alternative tool to overcome the limitations of experimental and other theoretical methods. AIMD directly monitors atomic motions of the system, allowing for all reactions to be observed in situ. However, time limitations and high energy barriers make reactions appear as rare events, occurring at longer timescales than accessible by AIMD. Enhanced sampling methods, which apply external bias potentials to a set of collective variables (CVs) encoding undersampled degrees of freedom of a system, are necessary to accelerate dynamics and achieve efficient exploration of reaction space. Our recent work proposes an automatic workflow for reaction discovery using a generic CV derived from spectral graph theory and the on-the-fly probability enhanced sampling method (OPESE). In this method, a molecule is represented as a graph whose vertices and edges correspond to its atoms and chemical bonds, respectively. The CV is the maximum eigenvalue (λmax) of the symmetric adjacency matrix associated with the graph, and fluctuations of λmax are enhanced by introducing bias from an on-the-fly estimation of the CV probability distribution in OPESE. Moreover, the bias deposited is controlled by a barrier cutoff, allowing control over the discovery phase. This approach delegates the task of finding new chemical pathways to the molecular dynamics engine, enabling a more thorough exploration of reaction space independent of user intuition. Thus, our method offers a more efficient and reliable means of discovering new chemical pathways.

Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations utilizing quantum mechanical force fields present a promising tool to overcome the limitations of experimental and theoretical methods. AIMD allows for the direct monitoring of atomic motions, enabling the observation of all reactions in situ. However, high energy barriers and time limitations result in reactions occurring at longer timescales than those accessible by AIMD. Therefore, enhanced sampling methods are necessary to accelerate dynamics and achieve efficient exploration of reaction space. By applying external bias potentials to a set of collective variables (CVs) encoding undersampled degrees of freedom, these methods allow for the acceleration of rare events. Our recent work presents an automatic workflow for reaction discovery using a CV derived from spectral graph theory and the on-the-fly probability enhanced sampling method (OPESE). A molecule is represented as a graph, with its vertices and edges corresponding to its atoms and chemical bonds. The CV is the maximum eigenvalue (λmax) of the symmetric adjacency matrix associated with the graph. The fluctuations of λmax are enhanced through an on-the-fly estimation of the CV probability distribution in OPESE. The bias is deposited and controlled by a barrier cutoff, allowing for thorough exploration of reaction space. This approach delegates the task of chemical pathway discovery to the molecular dynamics engine, independent of user intuition, and offers a more efficient and reliable means of discovering new pathways.

Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations, particularly those using ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) and quantum mechanical force fields, represent a promising alternative to experimental and theoretical methods. AIMD allows for the direct monitoring of atomic motions, enabling the observation of all reactions in situ. However, AIMD is limited by time constraints and high energy barriers, which can result in rare events occurring at longer timescales than accessible by AIMD. To address this challenge, enhanced sampling methods are necessary to accelerate dynamics and efficiently explore the reaction space. These methods apply external bias potentials to a set of collective variables (CVs) that encode undersampled degrees of freedom of a system. In this context, our recent work has proposed an automatic workflow for reaction discovery. The method employs a generic CV derived from spectral graph theory and the on-the-fly probability enhanced sampling method (OPESE). A molecule is represented as a graph, with each vertex and edge corresponding to an atom and a chemical bond, respectively. The CV is the maximum eigenvalue (λmax) of the symmetric adjacency matrix associated with the graph. The fluctuations of λmax are enhanced by introducing a bias based on an on-the-fly estimation of the CV probability distribution in OPESE. In addition, bias deposition is controlled by a barrier cutoff, which provides control over the discovery phase. This approach allows the molecular dynamics engine to explore reaction space comprehensively and without user intuition, thus providing a more efficient and reliable means of discovering new chemical pathways.

please help me to polish the following paragraph. requirements: 1. try to make the logic and language fluent and scientific 2. when polishing, try only to change the sentences’s order or replace phrases but do not delete any actual content. 3. note, it is very important not to delete any actual content when polishing. the paragraph to be polished is:A promising powerful alternative tool that overcomes the above disadvantages are molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. In particular, ab initio MD that use quantum mechanical force fields are especially useful in this regard. Ab initio MD directly monitor the atomic motion of the system, allowing for all the reactions to be directly observed in situ. However, the integration time step of MD is usually limited to femtoseconds or shorter (dictated by the fastest motion in the system), but the high energy barriers separating reactants and products make the reaction appear as rare events happening at much longer time scales (milliseconds or more) than ab initio MD can capture. Enhanced sampling methods are thus needed to accelerate the dynamics in simulations, where an external bias potential is applied to a set of carefully selected collective variables (CVs) that encode the generally undersampled slow degrees of freedom of a system. When proper CVs are identified, this procedure allows for the efficient exploration of reaction space and subsequent discovery of novel chemical pathways otherwise inaccessible on the timescale of AIMD. Recently, we proposed an automatic workflow for reaction discovery that relies on a generic CV derived from spectral graph theory and on the explore variant of the on-the-fly probability enhanced sampling method (OPESE). With our approach, a molecule is represented as a graph whose vertices and edges are its atoms and chemical bonds, respectively, and the CV is the maximum eigenvalue (λmax) of the symmetric adjacency matrix associated to the graph. In the OPESE method, λmax fluctuations are enhanced by introducing a bias from an on-the-fly estimation of the CV probability distribution. Furthermore, in OPESE, the bias deposited is controlled by a barrier cutoff, which sets a limit to the maximum value of the bias and allows control of the discovery phase. Thus, the task of finding new chemical pathways is completely left to the molecular dynamics engine, allowing for a more thorough exploration of the reaction space independent of a user’s chemical intuition.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, particularly those utilizing ab initio MD with quantum mechanical force fields, offer a promising and powerful alternative tool to address the limitations of experimental and other theoretical methods. By directly monitoring the atomic motion of the system, ab initio MD enables the observation of all reactions in situ. However, the integration time step of MD is often constrained to femtoseconds or shorter, dictated by the fastest motion in the system. High energy barriers separating reactants and products make reactions appear as rare events at much longer timescales (milliseconds or more) than ab initio MD can capture, necessitating enhanced sampling methods. In these methods, external bias potentials are applied to a set of collective variables (CVs) that encode the slow degrees of freedom of the system typically undersampled by standard MD simulations. By identifying proper CVs, enhanced sampling procedures facilitate the efficient exploration of reaction space and the discovery of new chemical pathways otherwise inaccessible on the timescale of AIMD. Our recent work proposes an automatic workflow for reaction discovery using a generic CV derived from spectral graph theory and the on-the-fly probability enhanced sampling method (OPESE). In this approach, a molecule is represented as a graph with vertices and edges corresponding to its atoms and chemical bonds, respectively. The CV is the maximum eigenvalue (λmax) of the symmetric adjacency matrix associated with the graph. The fluctuations of λmax are amplified through the introduction of bias from an on-the-fly estimation of the CV probability distribution in OPESE. Additionally, the bias deposited is controlled by a barrier cutoff, which sets a limit to the maximum value of the bias and allows for control of the discovery phase. Thus, the proposed method delegates the task of discovering new chemical pathways to the molecular dynamics engine, enabling thorough exploration of reaction space independent of the user's chemical intuition.

Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations utilizing quantum mechanical force fields present a promising and powerful alternative for overcoming the limitations of existing methods. While monitoring atomic motions of the system, AIMD simulations allow for the direct observation of potential reactions in situ. However, intrinsic limitations of MD simulations arise due to the statistical rarity of activated chemical reactions, restricting most AIMD simulations from fully exploring complete reaction spaces along the reaction coordinates or collective variables (CVs). This limitation is intrinsic to MD, as the time step used to discretize the time evolution must be smaller than the fastest motion in the system (typically femtoseconds or shorter). Meanwhile, high energy barriers separating reactants and products make reactions appear as rare events happening at a much longer time scale (milliseconds or more) than AIMD can capture.

Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations utilizing quantum mechanical force fields present a promising and powerful alternative for overcoming limitations of existing methods and the direct observation of potential reactions in situ. However, limitations arise due to the statistical rarity of activated chemical reactions, restricting most AIMD simulations from fully exploring complete reaction spaces along the reaction coordinates or collective variables (CVs). This intrinsic limitation arises because the time step used to discretize the time evolution in AIMD must be smaller than the fastest motion in the system (typically femtoseconds or shorter). Additionally, high energy barriers separating reactants and products make reactions appear as rare events happening at a much longer time scale (milliseconds or more) than AIMD can capture. To address these challenges, enhanced sampling (ES) methods are needed. ES methods accelerate the dynamics by applying external bias potentials to a set of carefully selected CVs, which encode the generally undersampled slow degrees of freedom in a reaction. In this regard, when proper CVs are defined, this procedure allows for the efficient exploration of reaction space and the subsequent discovery of novel chemical pathways otherwise inaccessible on the timescale of AIMD.
